summary: Deng Zhi; the Shu envoy to Wu. Walks calmly past the boiling cauldron and the lines of armed guards Sun Quan has set up to intimidate him. Delivers a coolheaded speech: Wu and Shu need each other against Wei. Sun Quan ratifies a fresh alliance. The chapter's diplomatic centerpiece.
Zhang Wen; Wu's return envoy to Shu; received with banquets in Chengdu; humiliated by Qin Mi's clever debating; converted into a respectful admirer of Shu.
Zhang Wen (duplicate record).
Qin Mi; the Shu wit who out-debates Zhang Wen at the welcoming banquet; gives the chapter's set-piece tour-de-force of erudition.
Sun Quan; receives Deng Zhi; reconciled with Shu; ends his Wei vassalage; takes the field again.

Cao Pi; furious at Sun Quan's defection back to Shu; mounts a great river invasion in person — embarks on warship-mounted dragon-boats at Guangling on the Yangtze opposite Wu's defences. Repulsed disastrously: Xu Sheng burns floating-rafts across the river that look like a wall of fire; Cao Pi's fleet panics.
Xu Sheng; the chapter's Wu hero. Defends Wu against Cao Pi's invasion at Guangling; builds the famous floating-fire-wall on the river that breaks Cao Pi's amphibious assault. Saves Wu in one move.
Ding Feng; with Xu Sheng's defence at the Yangtze; one of Cao Pi's pursuit officers Sun Shao spears off his horse.
Ding Feng (duplicate record).
Sun Shao; the young Wu commander who insists on intercepting Cao Pi's retreat; routs the rearguard. The hot-blooded counterpart to Xu Sheng's defensive caution.
Sun Shao (duplicate record).
Sun Shao (duplicate record).
Sun Ce; named in passing — Sun Quan's elder brother and founder.
Zhang Zhao; on Sun Quan's council.
Zhang Zhao (duplicate record).
Gu Yong; on Sun Quan's council.
Lu Xun; on Sun Quan's council; defends the Wuhu axis.
Liu Ye; on Cao Pi's council; urges caution against the Yangtze crossing — overruled.
Jia Xu; on Cao Pi's council.
Jia Xu (duplicate record).
Jiang Ji; advises against the invasion.
Sima Yi; in the Wei court — rising.
Xin Pi; in the Wei court.
Cao Zhen; with Cao Pi's invasion force.
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Ren; with Cao Pi's invasion force.
Cao Xiu; with Cao Pi's invasion force.
Xu Chu; with Cao Pi; rescues him in the rout.
Xu Huang; with Cao Pi.
Zhang He; with Cao Pi.
Zhang Liao; with Cao Pi; receives the arrow wound from Ding Feng that kills him after the retreat.
Wen Pin; with Cao Pi.
Zhao Yun; in Chengdu — receives the news of Wu's renewed alliance.
Zhuge Liang; orchestrates the Wu alliance; planning the southern campaign against Meng Huo.
Ma Chao; defends Hanzhong frontier.
Meng Da; in his Wei command at Xincheng; the Shu/Wu alliance shifts his position.
Meng Da (duplicate record).
Meng Huo; the Nanman king; named in passing — the chapter ends pointing south to the next campaign.
Wei Yan; with the Shu army.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Yong Kai; one of the southern rebels; named in passing — Zhuge Liang's coming target.
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.

Zhuge Liang; launches the Southern Campaign. Mobilises personally despite Wang Lian's objections about the dangerous Nanman terrain. Marches the Shu army south against Meng Huo. Takes the first three southern rebel commanders (Yong Kai, Zhu Bao, Gao Ding) one by one; turns Gao Ding to kill Yong Kai for him. Reaches the Nanman frontier.
Meng Huo; the Nanman king. First confronts Shu directly here; his initial defeat is the start of the seven-capture-seven-release sequence that will run through chapter 90.
Yong Kai; the senior southern rebel; killed by Gao Ding after Zhuge Liang's disinformation trick.
Zhu Bao; one of the three southern rebels; surrenders to Zhuge Liang.
Gao Ding; the third southern rebel; turned to Zhuge Liang's side; kills Yong Kai for him; accepts Shu service.
Wang Lian; in Liu Shan's court; warns Zhuge Liang against personally leading the southern expedition — the climate, the terrain, the alien tribes; brushed aside. Dies shortly after of grief.
Wang Ping; serves under Zhuge Liang in the southern campaign — his calm steadiness coming into its own as the campaign unfolds.
Ma Su; counsels Zhuge Liang at the campaign's start with the famous 'win their hearts, not their cities' (gōng xīn wèi shàng) — the strategic principle that frames the whole seven-capture sequence.
Zhao Yun; with the southern campaign — distinguished himself in the early engagements.
Wei Yan; with the southern campaign — distinguished himself in the early engagements.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao; with the southern campaign.
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Guan Xing; with the southern campaign.
Jiang Wan; Zhuge Liang's senior bureaucratic assistant on the campaign; future second-in-command after Zhuge Liang's death.
Ma Liang; with the campaign; the Yi-tribe expert.
Li Yan; ordered to defend the capital and the eastern frontier with Wu while Zhuge Liang is south.
Li Yan (duplicate record).
Lu Kai; Shu local-affairs official; advises Zhuge Liang on Nanman geography.
Cao Pi; off-stage in the north.
Sun Quan; off-stage in Wu.
Ma Chao; named in passing — already dead between chapters (died of illness around this time).
Lu Xun; in Wu.
Guan Yu; named in passing.
Fei Yi; in Liu Shan's court; rising; will be key in the next generation.
Dong Jue; in Liu Shan's court.
Fan Jian; in Liu Shan's court.
Wang Kang; minor figure named in passing.
Wang Kang (duplicate record).
Yu Zhong; minor figure named in passing.
Zhang Ni; with the southern campaign.
Zhang Yi; with the southern campaign.
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.

Zhuge Liang; the second and third captures of Meng Huo. Meng Huo is freed after the first capture; comes back; is captured a second time at the Lu River crossing; freed again; captured a third time when his subordinate Dong Tuna betrays him. Each time Meng Huo refuses to submit on the ground that he was outsmarted, not outfought. Zhuge Liang lets him go each time. The 'win his heart' strategy in slow motion.
Meng Huo; captured twice more in this chapter; freed twice more. Still defiant — claims each defeat was an unfair trick.
Zhao Yun; with the southern campaign — the spear-and-charge backbone of every engagement.
Wei Yan; with the southern campaign — feigned retreats his specialty.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Ma Dai; arrives with reinforcements at the Lu River; takes Meng Huo's son-in-law by stratagem.
Wang Ping; with the southern campaign.
Jiang Wan; in Zhuge Liang's staff.
Lu Kai; the chapter's terrain-and-water expert; guides Zhuge Liang's army across the malarial Lu River with safety advisories.
Ma Su; in Zhuge Liang's staff.
Sun Quan; off-stage.
Cao Cao; named in passing.
Zhang Liang; named in passing as historical Han-founding strategist allusion.
Zhang Liang (duplicate record).
Zhang Ni; with the campaign.
Zhang Yi; with the campaign.
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.

Zhuge Liang; the fourth and fifth captures. The Tuhua Spring of poisonous water nearly kills the Shu army (the four lethal springs: Dumb Spring, Mute Spring, Black Spring, Hot Spring) until Meng Jie — Meng Huo's elder brother — gives Zhuge Liang the cure (the *xiè yè cǎo* leaves). Then defeats King Mulu of the Bana Cave with his fire-breathing beasts using engineered wooden carved beasts that out-roar the originals. Meng Huo's wife Lady Zhurong is captured and exchanged. By chapter's end Meng Huo has been taken and released five times.
Meng Huo; captured a fourth and fifth time across the chapter — once via his betraying brother-in-law Yang Feng (whose tribe Meng Huo had abused), once via Lady Zhurong's exchange. Still defiant.
Zhao Yun; with the campaign.
Wei Yan; with the campaign; the chapter's chief feigned-retreat artist.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Ma Dai; with the campaign; captures Lady Zhurong in single combat.
Wang Ping; with the campaign.
Jiang Wan; on Zhuge Liang's staff.
Lu Kai; identifies the four lethal springs.
Liu Zhi; minor figure named in passing.
Liu Zhi (duplicate record).
Zhang Ni; with the campaign.
Zhang Yi; with the campaign.
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.

Zhuge Liang; the sixth and seventh captures. Burns the rattan-armoured army of King Wutugu of Wuge Cave in a sealed valley — the dreadful 'mulberry-coffin moment' when Zhuge Liang weeps as he watches 30,000 rattan-armoured men burn alive: 'My contribution to Heaven will be reduced because of this.' Captures Meng Huo a seventh and final time; this time, finally beaten in every category, Meng Huo prostrates himself and swears eternal loyalty. The whole south pacified. Zhuge Liang declines to garrison the territory — leaves the southern lands self-governing in the famous Han-Yi settlement that holds for the rest of the novel.

Meng Huo; captured for the seventh time; finally yields ungrudgingly with the famous line, weeping, 'You captured and released me seven times — never has there been such grace; I will not turn my back on it.' Confirmed king of the south under Shu suzerainty.
Zhao Yun; with the campaign through the climactic burnings.
Wei Yan; with the campaign — feigns retreat across the valley that draws the rattan-armoured army into the fire-trap.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Ma Dai; with the campaign.
Fei Yi; the chapter records he is sent ahead with administrative reports.
Lu Kai; with the campaign — terrain expert through the burning-valley operation.
Zhang Ni; with the campaign.
Zhang Yi; with the campaign.
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).
Zhang Yi (duplicate record).

Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.
